title: Rourea dictyophylla
intro: Rourea dictyophylla, also known as "Dok Ding" or "Rourea," is a plant species native to Southeast Asia, belonging to the family Connaraceae. The plant has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which can help alleviate conditions such as fever, cough, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Rourea dictyophylla include alkaloids, flavonoids, and phenolic compounds, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, Rourea dictyophylla is often prepared as a decoction or infusion, and is also used in the form of a topical cream or ointment to treat skin conditions and wounds.
taxonomy: Rourea dictyophylla is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Ochnaceae. This family is part of the order Ochnales, which includes several other plant families. The order Ochnales is also part of the clade Rosidae, a large group of flowering plants. The clade Rosidae is a subclass of the class Magnoliopsida, which contains most of the world's flowering plants. The kingdom of Plantae encompasses all plant species, with Rourea dictyophylla being a part of this kingdom.
